title: Image & Text in the Same Space
concept: CLIP-style joint embeddings enabling text-to-image and image-to-image search over one shared vector space.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned about multilingual retrieval. What is a key challenge when searching across different languages. What is a major challenge for low-resource languages with less-oblem multilingual retrieval. What is a major challenge when searching across-what is a main challenge for lower-resource languages?

options.text: Low-resource languages are always translated to English before search.
Uneven training data across languages can skew search results and lower quality.
All languages perform identically because vector spaces are purely math.

exercises.explain: Low-resource languages often suffer from lower retrieval quality because models have less training data to accurately map their vectors.

cards.title: Bridging the Visual-Text Gap
cards.body: How do computers know that a photo of a fluffy cat and the written word 'feline' mean the same thing? Traditionally, they couldn't unless we manually tagged every image. Joint embeddings change everything.

cards.title: What is a Joint Embedding Space?
cards.body: Joint embeddings project different types of data—like text and images—into a single shared vector space. In this space, an image of a cat and the written word 'cat' sit right next to each other because they share the same meaning.

cards.title: The Universal Concept Dictionary
cards.body: Think of a bilingual dictionary where both 'dog' and a picture of a puppy point to the exact same page. By mapping words and images to the same location, we can find a picture using words without needing text tags!

cards.title: Searching Without Tags
cards.body: If you search a joint database for 'sunny beach', the system retrieves photos of sandy shores and oceans, even if those photos have absolutely no text descriptions or filenames attached to them.

cards.title: Myth: Images Must Be Labeled with Text
cards.body: Many think AI must first convert an image into a text caption to search it. In reality, joint models convert both the raw pixels and the query text directly into math vectors that can be compared instantly.

cards.title: Visualizing the Shared Space
cards.body: See how different inputs are mapped to the same conceptual region in a joint embedding space.

left.text: The typed sentence 'A red sports car speeding down the highway'

right.text: A photograph of a crimson race car on asphalt with motion blur

exercises.prompt: How does a model like CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training) align text and images?

options.text: It translates all images into Spanish first.
It uses separate encoders for text and images that output to the same vector space.
It ignores the images and only indexes text documents.
It manually hires humans to tag every incoming image.

exercises.explain: CLIP uses two separate encoders (one for images, one for text) that output vectors into the exact same multi-dimensional space, allowing direct comparison.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A joint embedding space allows you to perform image-to-image search as well as text-to-image search.
exercises.explain: In a joint embedding space, you can compare image-to-image, text-to-image, or text-to-text because all vectors live in the same coordinate system.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blanks to describe where images and text are placed to enable search:
exercises.sentence: To search photos using words, we project both into a shared vector space.
exercises.explain: By embedding both images and text into a shared vector space, we can mathematically compare their positions to find matches.

exercises.prompt: Which of these search tools does NOT use a joint multimodal embedding space?

options.text: An application where you upload a photo of a jacket to find similar-looking jackets in an online store.
A search bar where typing 'peaceful forest' instantly retrieves unlabelled drone footage of pine trees.
A search tool that only returns images if the filename contains the exact word you typed.

exercises.explain: The third option is typical of traditional tag-based search engines, whereas the first two represent advanced AI applications using joint text-image embeddings.

exercises.prompt: You are building a search engine for a stock photo website using a CLIP joint embedding model. When a user uploads a photo of a 'golden retriever' or types the words 'golden retriever', how does the system find matches?

options.text: The text vector for 'golden retriever' and the image vector of a golden retriever will be very close together in the shared space.
The system translates the image into a text caption first, then searches the database.
Images and text are stored in two completely separate databases that cannot interact.
It only works if the image file has a metadata tag matching the text query.

exercises.explain: CLIP aligns text and images into a single vector space. A text query like 'yellow submarine' will have a vector close to the vector of an actual image of a yellow submarine, enabling direct cross-modal search.
